For each category: project GMV using a bottoms-up model (seller count × average GMV per active seller × seasonal adjustment), then apply the expected take rate by seller tier (enterprise vs. SMB vs. consumer) and promo calendar. Aggregate across categories to get total net revenue. Variance analysis in the monthly ops review should show actuals vs. model at the category level, not just total — this lets you isolate whether a miss is driven by GMV shortfall or take rate compression. Use dbt + Snowflake or BigQuery for the data pipeline and Looker or Tableau for the executive dashboard. Review the model assumptions quarterly and update take rate expectations when new contracts are signed or promo programs are changed.

q: You're a Principal tasked with deciding whether to sunset a $30M ARR product that is growing 5% YoY but consuming 20% of R&D. How do you make this recommendation?
a: This is a resource allocation and portfolio decision requiring a structured analysis across four dimensions. (1) Financial contribution: At $30M ARR, 5% growth, and assuming 70% gross margin, the product generates $21M gross profit annually — this is meaningful cash contribution. However, at 20% R&D allocation (e.g., $15M in a $75M R&D budget), the product's R&D ROI is $21M gross profit ÷ $15M R&D = 1.4× — which is poor vs. a high-growth product at 5× or more. (2) Strategic portfolio role: Is this product a standalone, or does it serve as a retention anchor or cross-sell engine for the core business? If 40% of your enterprise customers use this product, sunsetting it triggers churn risk that must be quantified: 40% × customer base × average ACV × estimated churn rate. (3) Alternative uses of the 20% R&D: quantify the opportunity cost — if redirected to your fastest-growing product, what is the incremental ARR potential? A 20% R&D increase to the core product might accelerate growth from 40% to 55%, adding $50M ARR over 3 years. (4) Sunset options: Full sunset (migration required), harvest mode (minimal maintenance, no new features), or divest (sell to a PE firm or strategic acquirer). Harvest mode recovers the cash contribution with minimal R&D while removing the innovation opportunity cost. Present the board with the full option set and quantified trade-offs, not just 'keep or kill.'
fj: Candidates treat a low-growth legacy product as a binary 'keep vs. kill' decision without modeling the harvest option, divestiture value, and customer churn risk of sunsetting.
fs: Legacy product portfolio decisions have four options — invest, maintain, harvest, and divest — and the financially optimal path depends on the opportunity cost of R&D reallocation and the customer churn risk embedded in the existing customer base.

followups.q: You decide to sunset the product. How do you manage the customer migration without triggering mass churn?
followups.a: Announce sunset 12–18 months in advance — industry standard for enterprise SaaS is at least 12 months' notice. Provide a clear migration path to either the core product or a partner/alternative, with a migration team (CSM + Solutions Engineers) assigned to each Tier 1 account. Offer financial incentives: waive migration fees, provide free professional services for migration, and offer a 1-year price lock on the new product. Assign an executive sponsor to the 20 largest customers. Track migration progress monthly in the BizOps scorecard — any customer 6 months from sunset without a confirmed migration path is a churn risk that needs CEO-level attention. Set retention guardrails: if migration rate is below 60% at the 9-month mark, evaluate extending the sunset date or offering a legacy maintenance contract at premium pricing.

followups.q: A competitor acquires your sunset product's competitor and re-invests aggressively. Does this change your recommendation?
followups.a: Yes, this is a strategic signal that requires reassessment. The relevant questions: Why did the competitor see value you're missing? Is there a market shift (e.g., regulatory tailwind, new use case) that your internal analysis underweighted? Does the competitor's acquisition give them a cross-sell advantage against your customer base? In the short term, pause the sunset announcement and rerun the strategic analysis with the new competitive context. Commission a rapid competitive intelligence review (2–3 weeks) to understand the competitor's investment thesis. If the competitor is buying the product to sell into their large enterprise customer base, it may become a churn accelerator for you — the case for harvest or divestiture strengthens, not weakens. If they see a new market opportunity you missed, the case for reinvestment may emerge.

followups.q: How do you value the product if you decide to divest it?
followups.a: Divestiture value depends on the acquirer type. Strategic acquirer (a company with complementary products or distribution): typically pays 3–5× ARR for a product in harvest mode, more if there's growth potential they can unlock with their sales motion — at $30M ARR, strategic value is $90M–$150M. Financial acquirer (PE firm): typically pays 1.5–2.5× ARR for a cash-generating, low-growth SaaS product — $45M–$75M. The market condition matters: in 2021–2022, multiples were 6–10× ARR; in 2023–2025, they compressed to 2–5× for low-growth assets. Run a clean data room (financial model, customer contracts, product documentation, team roster) and approach 3–5 potential acquirers through a structured process. Your investment bankers (or Corp Dev team internally) manage the process; BizOps supports with data and analysis. The decision to divest vs. harvest hinges on whether the lump sum proceeds, invested in core growth, yield more NPV than the 5-year harvest cash flow.
